claim: Incidence rates of cervical cancer have increased due to nationwide screening programs based primarily on cytology to detect uterine cervical cancer.

result.claim: Incidence rates of cervical cancer have increased due to nationwide screening programs based primarily on cytology to detect uterine cervical cancer.
result.reasoning: The claim that incidence rates of cervical cancer have increased due to nationwide screening programs based primarily on cytology is strongly refuted by the available evidence. Paper ID 36355784, "The effect of mass screening on incidence and mortality of squamous and adenocarcinoma of cervix uteri," explicitly states that in Finland, following the implementation of a nationwide mass screening program in the mid-1960s, the mean incidence of cervical carcinoma significantly decreased from 15.4 per 10^5 woman-years in the early 1960s to 2.7 per 10^5 woman-years in 1991. This paper concludes that the Finnish mass screening program has been effective. Similarly, Paper ID 25742130, "Mass screening programmes and trends in cervical cancer in Finland and the Netherlands," compares trends in cervical cancer incidence and mortality in Finland and the Netherlands, both of which have national screening programs. This paper reports that incidence and mortality rates have declined in both countries, with a more pronounced decline in Finland. These findings consistently demonstrate a decrease, not an increase, in cervical cancer incidence rates in countries with established cytology-based screening programs.

evidence.text: HPV screening provides a longer low-risk period than cytology.
evidence.source: Efficacy of human papillomavirus testing for the detection of invasive cervical cancers and cervical intraepithelial neoplasia: a randomised controlled trial.

evidence.text: Implementation of HPV DNA testing in cervical screening leads to earlier detection of clinically relevant CIN grade 2 or worse.
evidence.source: Human papillomavirus testing for the detection of high-grade cervical intraepithelial neoplasia and cancer: final results of the POBASCAM randomised controlled trial.

evidence.text: The study compared the incidence of cervical cancer in Finland and the Netherlands.
evidence.source: Mass screening programmes and trends in cervical cancer in Finland and the Netherlands.

evidence.text: The primary endpoint was the detection of grade 2 and 3 CIN, and of invasive cervical cancers during the first and second screening rounds.
evidence.source: Efficacy of human papillomavirus testing for the detection of invasive cervical cancers and cervical intraepithelial neoplasia: a randomised controlled trial.

evidence.text: Seven cases of invasive cervical cancer were detected in the HPV group during the first round of screening.
evidence.source: Efficacy of human papillomavirus testing for the detection of invasive cervical cancers and cervical intraepithelial neoplasia: a randomised controlled trial.

evidence.text: The study assessed whether HPV DNA testing in the first screen decreases detection of cervical intraepithelial neoplasia (CIN) grade 3 or worse, CIN grade 2 or worse, and cervical cancer in the second screening.
evidence.source: Human papillomavirus testing for the detection of high-grade cervical intraepithelial neoplasia and cancer: final results of the POBASCAM randomised controlled trial.

evidence.text: Cervical cancer incidence and mortality data were obtained from the Finnish Cancer Registry.
evidence.source: The effect of mass screening on incidence and mortality of squamous and adenocarcinoma of cervix uteri.

evidence.text: 18 invasive cancers were detected in the cytology group over the two rounds of screening.
evidence.source: Efficacy of human papillomavirus testing for the detection of invasive cervical cancers and cervical intraepithelial neoplasia: a randomised controlled trial.

evidence.text: HPV-based screening is more effective than cytology in preventing invasive cervical cancer.
evidence.source: Efficacy of human papillomavirus testing for the detection of invasive cervical cancers and cervical intraepithelial neoplasia: a randomised controlled trial.

evidence.text: The incidence of cervical carcinoma was 2.7 per 10(5) woman-years in 1991.
evidence.source: The effect of mass screening on incidence and mortality of squamous and adenocarcinoma of cervix uteri.

evidence.text: Participants in the cervical screening programme in the Netherlands were randomly assigned to receive HPV DNA (GP5+/6+-PCR method) and cytology co-testing or cytology testing alone.
evidence.source: Human papillomavirus testing for the detection of high-grade cervical intraepithelial neoplasia and cancer: final results of the POBASCAM randomised controlled trial.

evidence.text: The relative detection (HPV vs cytology) was 2.03 for CIN2 and 3 together among women aged 35-60 years during round one screening.
evidence.source: Efficacy of human papillomavirus testing for the detection of invasive cervical cancers and cervical intraepithelial neoplasia: a randomised controlled trial.

evidence.text: In 2003, the age-adjusted incidence of cervical cancer in the Netherlands was 4.9 per 100,000 woman-years.
evidence.source: Mass screening programmes and trends in cervical cancer in Finland and the Netherlands.

evidence.text: At enrollment, the proportion of women in the intervention group who were found to have lesions of grade 2 or 3 cervical intraepithelial neoplasia or cancer was 51% greater than the proportion of women in the control group who were found to have such lesions.
evidence.source: Human papillomavirus and Papanicolaou tests to screen for cervical cancer.

evidence.text: Nine cases of invasive cervical cancer were detected in the cytology group during the first round of screening.
evidence.source: Efficacy of human papillomavirus testing for the detection of invasive cervical cancers and cervical intraepithelial neoplasia: a randomised controlled trial.

evidence.text: Combined HPV and cytology screening provides a further small gain in sensitivity at the expense of a considerable loss in specificity if positive by either test is referred to colposcopy, in comparison with HPV testing only.
evidence.source: Evidence regarding human papillomavirus testing in secondary prevention of cervical cancer.

evidence.text: Cervical cancer was less common in the intervention group (four of 19 579) than in the control group (14 of 19,731).
evidence.source: Human papillomavirus testing for the detection of high-grade cervical intraepithelial neoplasia and cancer: final results of the POBASCAM randomised controlled trial.

evidence.text: In 2003, the age-adjusted incidence of cervical cancer in Finland was 4.0 per 100,000 woman-years.
evidence.source: Mass screening programmes and trends in cervical cancer in Finland and the Netherlands.

evidence.text: The mean incidence of cervical carcinoma in the early 1960s was 15.4 per 10(5) woman-years.
evidence.source: The effect of mass screening on incidence and mortality of squamous and adenocarcinoma of cervix uteri.

evidence.text: Nine cases of invasive cervical cancer were detected in the cytology group during the second round of screening.
evidence.source: Efficacy of human papillomavirus testing for the detection of invasive cervical cancers and cervical intraepithelial neoplasia: a randomised controlled trial.
